title: Dyeing with bog myrtle
subtitle: A strong, resinous yellow from Myrica gale leaves on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Bog myrtle (Myrica gale), also called sweet gale, is a native British shrub that produces a strong, warm yellow on alum-mordanted wool. The leaves are harvested in summer when the resinous oils are highest; the dye bath has a distinctive aromatic scent.
sourceNotes: Bog myrtle (Myrica gale) as a natural dye documented in Jenny Dean, Wild Colour (1999, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in historical Scottish and Scandinavian plant-dye literature. The plant is traditionally associated with Scottish Highland dyeing practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that bonds the dye to the wool fibre and fixes the colour. Alum is the standard mordant for bog myrtle.
The extracted dye liquid into which the mordanted fibre is placed to absorb colour.

A plant compound that can act as a weak mordant for protein fibres or as a pre-mordant to prepare plant fibres for dyeing. Bog myrtle contains natural tannin that enhances dye uptake even without additional mordant.

content.text: Bog myrtle makes a stronger, more resinous
than most foraged plant dyes. Its natural
content means the colour takes well even with a light
. Harvest only from plants you can identify confidently and in small quantities, leaving the plant to regenerate.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots and utensils are dedicated. Wear gloves and apron. Work outdoors or with ventilation. Alum is low-hazard; keep away from food.

items.name: Fresh bog myrtle leaves (Myrica gale, picked in summer)

content.text: Mordant the wool with alum at 80 degrees Celsius for 45 minutes. Cool in the bath. Bog myrtle's tannin content is sufficient to produce colour without mordant, but alum gives a clearer, brighter yellow; unmordanted dyeing produces a more muted beige-tan.

Place the fresh leaves in the dye-pot with water to cover and bring to a simmer. Hold at 80 to 90 degrees Celsius for 45 minutes. The bath will turn a strong amber-yellow and will smell distinctly aromatic and resinous. Strain through a cloth into a clean pot.
Add the mordanted, damp fibre to the dye bath. Raise slowly to 80 degrees Celsius and hold for 45 to 60 minutes. Remove and rinse in progressively cooler water until clear.
The alum mordant bath and bog myrtle dye bath can be composted or poured down the foul drain in small home quantities.

An iron after-bath darkens the yellow toward a warm olive. A copper after-bath produces a deeper, more complex gold-green. Both are stronger modifiers on bog myrtle than on many other plant dyes because of its high tannin content.

items.problem: Only pale beige colour was achieved despite using a large quantity of plant material
items.cause: Leaves were harvested too late in the season (autumn) when the resinous oil content has declined
items.fix: Harvest bog myrtle in early to mid-summer for the strongest colour. Autumn leaves produce significantly less dye.
